document_chunks[4].chunk_text: Comment on project number agreement (Xenium) All Xenium analysis/meeting
files will be stored as Project 34, as this is a collaboration and it is
easier to have it separate for data sharing across projects/labs.
Experimentally, we will handle the pre/post xenium cycif as #4, since
many of the antibodies and experimental setting are related to the cell
cycle project and it is easier to track from the wet-lab side

[Comment from 03-09-2025]{.underline}

Blocks to be used in Xenium experiment will get destroyed to get part of
the tissue out and accommodate it to new blocks. That is why Saun got
some serial sections (6 per block) - that we will use in other
experiments. Out of these sections:

- #1 is used for HE to assess tissue quality and location of tumor
 > mass

- #3 is used for Expansion CycIF

- #6 is used for tCycIF pre-screening

### Clinical data for the Expansion project

- :L:\\Projects\\4_CellCycle\\clinical_data\\Expansion\\cleaned_data

document_chunks[6].chunk_text: **Welcome to the Cell Cycle project!!**

The objective is to study the CellCycle IDS samples, primarily HRP, with
some HRD samples included. CycIF experiments were conducted and analyzed
for this cohort, enabling the identification of multiple cell
phenotypes---immune, stromal, and tumor---based on specific markers.
Additionally, functional markers related to the cell cycle, such as
cyclins, p21, Ki67, and PCNA, were examined. The study also encompasses
tumor meta-clustering and spatial analysis. Furthermore, we are
developing a technique to isolate cells using the RareCyte scanner,
followed by mass spectrometry analysis (results are available in this
folder).

Project lead: Ada Junquera

document_chunks[10].chunk_text: HGSC is often diagnosed at advanced stages due to the lack of effective
screening strategies, together with unspecific symptomatology.
First-line chemotherapy regimens have evolved over the past three
decades. Single-agent melphalan chemotherapy was initially replaced, in
the 1970s, by the combination of **cyclophosphamide with the
anthracycline doxorubicin** and, in the 1980s, was replaced by
concurrent **cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin and cisplatin**, or
**cyclophosphamide and cisplatin only**.
[[Carboplatin]{.underline}]{.mark} was also introduced in the 1980s and
was found to have comparable efficacy to cisplatin, but with
significantly less toxicity. In the early 1990s, **paclitaxel** was
shown to be the most effective agent in patients with relapsed
platinum-refractory disease, and this established its role in the
treatment of ovarian cancer. Since then, a number of trials have
evaluated whether paclitaxel can contribute to platinum-based first-line
chemotherapy.
([[https://www.nature.com/articles/nrc1123]{.underline}](https://www.nature.com/articles/nrc1123))

**Carboplatin** acts similarly to cisplatin but with less side-effects

- It has a bidentate dicarboxylate (CBDCA) ligand in place of the two

In the 1980s Carboplatin tested to be effective in ovarian cancer
treatment

[TAXANES: PACLITAXEL]{.underline}

**Paclitaxel targets β-tubulin**. Paclitaxel-treated cells have defects
in mitotic spindle assembly, chromosome segregation, and cell division.
Chromosomes are unable to achieve a metaphase spindle configuration.
Prolonged activation of the mitotic checkpoint triggers apoptosis or
reversion to the G0-phase of the cell cycle without cell division. In
addition, taxane induces cancer cell death by activating the cleavage of
procaspases and proapoptotic markers.

[It limits the development of cancer cells by causing a stop in the cell
cycle phases, specifically at the G2/M phase]{.underline}

**Treatment combination:**

1996: A phase III clinical trial led by Dr. Robert J. C. T. at
Gynecologic Oncology Group (GOG) tested the combination of carboplatin +
paclitaxel in women with advanced ovarian cancer. The trial compared
this combination to older regimens that used cyclophosphamide and
cisplatin.

 > survival rates for patients receiving the carboplatin-paclitaxel
 > combination. **Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall
 > survival (OS) were notably better** compared to the older
 > regimens. It showed a higher response rate (i.e., the percentage
 > of patients whose tumors shrank or disappeared).

- This trial was a game changer. It not only confirmed the efficacy of
 > the combination but also established carboplatin and paclitaxel as
 > the standard of care for first-line treatment of ovarian cancer,
 > including HGSC.

- By the early 2000s, clinical practice guidelines from major cancer
 > organizations such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network

ment of ovarian cancer,
 > including HGSC.

- By the early 2000s, clinical practice guidelines from major cancer
 > organizations such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network
 > (NCCN) and the European Society of Gynaecological Oncology (ESGO)
 > endorsed carboplatin and paclitaxel as the standard first-line
 > treatment for ovarian cancer, particularly for advanced stages

Over time, while **carboplatin-paclitaxel** remains the backbone of
treatment, **maintenance therapy** with newer agents like
**Bevacizumab** (anti-angiogenesis) and **PARP inhibitors** (for
patients with BRCA mutations) have been added to help extend remission
and improve survival.

**Chemotherapy resistance:**

Resistance can into primary and secondary:

1. primary is caused by tumor cells before therapy exposure

2. secondary is tumor adaptation to the treatment, eg high expression
 > of target proteins

Breast, ovarian, gastric, and uterine cancers become chemo-resistant
after being treated with chemotherapy for a long time, especially
paclitaxel.

document_chunks[15].chunk_text: **What is cell cycle?**

Cell cycle is the process through which a cell is able to effectively
divide

**Cell cycle and antitumor immunity**

**Senescence**

Multiple events in the microenvironment, as DNA damage or deprivation of
nutrients can induce senesgence. Senesence is a form of arrest in with
not only the cell division is shut down, but also triggers the SASP
(senescence-associated secretory phenotype), linked to secretion of
multiple characteristic proteins (such as chemokines, cytokines or
proteases). Depending on the biological context, senescence could limit
or support the tumor progression:

Within cancer cells

- SASP may facilitate the proliferative signalling of the surrounding
 > cancer cells

- Senescence may be just a transitory state, possibly associated with
 > therapy resistance. Since the treatments often target
 > proliferating cells, this form of dormancy might confer protective
 > effects to tumor cells.

CAFs (Cancer associated fibroblasts) can become senescent

**[How is senescence traced?]{.underline}**

, this form of dormancy might confer protective
 > effects to tumor cells.

CAFs (Cancer associated fibroblasts) can become senescent

**[How is senescence traced?]{.underline}**

> Traits associated in ovca with cancer cell senescence (with doxo
> treatment) are:

- expression of senescence-associated ß-galactosidase

- micronuclei

- G1-arrest

Many researchers target for SA b-galactosidase + p21 (p16INK4A)
expression as well as IL-6 and IL-1alpha

### Background: 

Here's a categorized list of genes for Cell Cycle Dysregulation,
Checkpoint Dysfunction, and Proliferation Markers:

1\. Cell Cycle Dysregulation

These genes are key regulators of the G1/S and G2/M transitions, often
altered in high-grade serous ovarian cancer.

- TP53: Frequently mutated; regulates cell cycle arrest.

- BRCA1/BRCA2: Implicated in homologous recombination (HR); loss
 > affects G2/M progression.

- CCNE1: Amplification disrupts G1/S transition control.

- RB1: Loss of function removes G1/S checkpoint control.

- CDKN2A/CDKN2B: Loss or mutation results in unchecked CDK4/6
 > activity.

- MYC: Amplified; drives G1/S progression.

- FOXM1: Overexpressed transcription factor critical for cell cycle
 > regulation.

2\. Checkpoint Dysfunction

These genes are crucial for DNA damage response and maintaining proper
cell cycle checkpoints.

cription factor critical for cell cycle

2\. Checkpoint Dysfunction

These genes are crucial for DNA damage response and maintaining proper
cell cycle checkpoints.

- ATM/ATR: DNA damage sensors; mutations impair G1/S and G2/M
 > checkpoint activation.

- CHK1/CHK2: Mediators of checkpoint signaling; mutations lead to cell
 > cycle checkpoint bypass.

- BUB1/BUB1B: Spindle assembly checkpoint regulators; mutations result
 > in chromosomal instability.

- AURKA/AURKB: Regulate mitotic spindle formation and chromosomal
 > segregation.

- PLK1: Involved in spindle assembly and mitotic progression.

- RAD51: Key for DNA repair; dysfunction leads to checkpoint failure.

3\. Proliferation Markers

These genes are often overexpressed, driving unchecked tumor growth and
aggressive proliferation.

- FOXM1: Master regulator of mitotic genes and cell cycle-related
 > processes.

- PLK1: Promotes centrosome maturation and spindle formation;
 > overexpression supports mitosis.

- TPX2: Enhances spindle assembly; often amplified.

- MYC: Potent driver of proliferative signaling pathways

unk_text: [Sample sectioning]{.underline}

Tissue samples were processed into paraffin blocks by the tissue
preparation and histochemistry unit (TPHU) of the Anatomy Department in
Biomedicum. Of those formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) blocks 10
µm thick tissue sections were sliced with a Microm HM 355 rotary
microtome, placed on SuperFrost micros 10cope glass slides and dried by
first baking them in the oven at 56°C for 2 h for and then leaving them
at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards they were stored at 4°C until
further usage.

[tCycIF]{.underline}

*Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre-staining*

First, slides were baked at 56°C for 30 min to ensure the tissue was
properly attached to the slide. Then, all slides were deparaffinized and
rehydrated by placing them first three times in xylene for 5 min each
followed by immersing them for 3 min each in a series of ethanol
solution with gradually decreasing ethanol concentration (100%, 90%,
70%, 50%, and 30%). Finally, the slides were washed twice for 1 min in
1x Tris-buffered saline (TBS) buffer (50 mM Tris-Cl, pH 7.6; 150 mM
NaCl).

In a next step, the slides were submerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid
buffer, pH 6.0, and heated up to 95°C for 15 min in an antigen retrieval
chamber with a pressure cooker. Afterwards, the slides in citric acid

ubmerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid
buffer, pH 6.0, and heated up to 95°C for 15 min in an antigen retrieval
chamber with a pressure cooker. Afterwards, the slides in citric acid
buffer were taken out of the cooker to cool down for 20 min at room
temperature and then they were washed three times for 1 min each in 1x
TBS buffer. This is done to fully retrieve the antigen molecules, which
can be slightly modified and cross-linked after the formalin fixation.

To minimize non-specific antibody binding of the tissue, 200-300 µL of
Odyssey blocking buffer were pipetted onto the tissue samples, which
were then incubated for 30 min in a moist incubation chamber to prevent
evaporation and dehydration of the tissue. Afterwards the Odyssey
blocking buffer was removed by vertically tapping the slides on a tissue
paper. Then the slides were washed three times for 3 min in 1x TBS
buffer.

*Antibody staining*

Before the actual staining was performed an antibody panel was designed
that could be applied in three cycles. The antibodies were selected to
detect tumor, stromal and immune cells as well as different markers of
the cell cycle.

For each cycle of staining, an antibody mastermix was prepared
containing all antibodies for one cycle and HOECHST diluted accordingly
in Odyssey blocking buffer. Per slide 100 µL of the antibody mixture

ng, an antibody mastermix was prepared
containing all antibodies for one cycle and HOECHST diluted accordingly
in Odyssey blocking buffer. Per slide 100 µL of the antibody mixture
were pipetted onto the tissue sample and the slides were incubated in a
moist incubation chamber at 4°C overnight. Since only dye-conjugated
antibodies were chosen for the antibody panel no secondary antibodies
were necessary on the next day and the slides were directly washed three
times in 1x TBS for 3 min each. Afterwards, 80-100 µL of 70% glycerol
were pipetted onto the tissue samples and the slides were covered with a
coverslip for the following imaging. After the imaging the coverslip is
removed again by placing the slides vertically in 1x TBS. After a short

min at room temperature. After 45 min, the bleaching solution was
replaced with fresh one. Afterwards, the slides were washed four times
in 1xTBS for 3 min each.

[Image acquisition, processing, and visualization]{.underline}

*Image acquisition*

The images of the tissue samples were acquired using a Rarecyte
CyteFinder® II high-throughput scanner. This is an automated microscope
platform for digital imaging that enables multiplexed, high resolution
tissue imaging of the whole slide. It contains a slide holder with space
for two slides and multiple microscope lenses for scanning the slides at
different magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, 40x). With its different

de. It contains a slide holder with space
for two slides and multiple microscope lenses for scanning the slides at
different magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, 40x). With its different
channels (488 nm, 555 nm, 647 nm, 750 nm) up to four antibodies can be
scanned simultaneously. Additionally, in each cycle Hoechst 33342 is
scanned at 361 nm. Hoechst is used for nuclear staining, and it is
especially important to stain and image Hoechst in each cycle for the
image processing afterwards. The scanner is connected to a series of
software to control and perform the imaging. The RareCyte Instrument
Controller helps to operate the microscope, the CyteHub is a control
center for scanning assays and image analysis, with the CyteFinder the
scan process can be set up and monitored, and the Imager allows
real-time imaging and picking control (see chapter picking).

The first step of the imaging process was placing the covered slides in
the slide holder upside down since the microscope lenses are inverted
and positioned beneath the slide holder. Then, a scanning assay needed
to be created in CyteHub. In it all necessary parameters for the
scanning process were defined - slide type ("custom ROI"), sample type
("tissue"), illumination type ("fluorescence"), imaging settings such as
magnification type ("10x" for slides from which after the imaging cells

e type ("custom ROI"), sample type
("tissue"), illumination type ("fluorescence"), imaging settings such as
magnification type ("10x" for slides from which after the imaging cells
were picked), binning ("1", which merges pixels and reduces resolution),
and scan overlap (20 µm for the X and Y overlap), focus settings,
stitching settings for defining which channel the stitching of the
images is based on afterwards ("BV421" which is the channel of Hoechst),
and scan channels, which assign the used antibodies and a suitable
exposure time to their respective channels. To test which exposure time
is appropriate for each antibody the Imager software was used
beforehand. After the assay was created, the scanning of the slides was
started using CyteFinder. The first time a slide was scanned a pre-scan
needed to be done that identified the tissue area on the slide. This
allowed then to manually adjust the scanning area and select focus
points for the area to be scanned, which is necessary for a successful
overlay of the various images from different cycles of one slide. The
chosen settings were stored in CyteHub and were applied to every future
scan of the slide.

The actual scanning process was performed automatically. For this, the
area to be scanned was divided into a grid of micro-regions, so called
"tiles", by the system. The tiles had overlapping regions with their

performed automatically. For this, the
area to be scanned was divided into a grid of micro-regions, so called
"tiles", by the system. The tiles had overlapping regions with their
adjacent neighbour tiles to ensure a correct stitching of the tiles
during the image processing. The number of tiles depended on the chosen
magnification. The tiles were scanned one after another and only when
all channels of one tile were scanned, the scanning of the next tile
started. After the whole selected area was scanned the raw data was
stored as a .rcpnl file for further processing.

document_chunks[27].chunk_text: To assemble all tiles of all channels and images of one slide, the
open-source Python package ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous
Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency Registration) was used to process the
generated .rcpnl files. Its workflow consists mainly of two steps,
stitching and registration, and is based on the nuclear staining of the
Hoechst channel, since the nuclei are objects with high contrast, well
defined positions, and clear boundaries. In the stitching step adjacent

based on the nuclear staining of the
Hoechst channel, since the nuclei are objects with high contrast, well
defined positions, and clear boundaries. In the stitching step adjacent
tiles of each imaging cycle were aligned, while during the registration
tiles from different imaging cycles that cover the same region were
aligned. Running the ASHLAR programme results in the generation of an
ome.tiff file, a multichannel pyramidal stacked image consisting of
multiple layers with different resolutions. This format quickens
visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only
loading a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each
moment.

*Image visualization*

With Napari.

[Cell picking from the scanned slides]{.underline}

ROIs selection

From the antibody-stained slides three types of cells were picked, tumor
cells in M-phase, tumor cells in S-phase, and stromal cells. The
identification of corresponding cells was based on the fluorescence
intensity of different biomarkers. Tumor cells were recognized by PanCK
and PAX8. Stroma cells were identified by the biomarker Vimentin. Tumor
cells that expressed pH3 were chosen for the M-phase cells, while cell
cycle biomarker pRb marked the S-phase cells.

For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the imaging results (ome.tiff file)
were opened in the CyteHub software in the tissue result section. By

marker pRb marked the S-phase cells.

For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the imaging results (ome.tiff file)
were opened in the CyteHub software in the tissue result section. By
turning on the channels of the respective biomarkers the desired cells
were identified and marked with circles with a 40 µm diameter which
corresponds to the diameter of the microneedle that was used for the
picking. The selected ROIs were stored in CyteHub as a rcpickmap file.

*Picking preparation*

First, the needle holder was prepared. Next to space for six needles it
can hold six tubes and has a built-in needle discard tray. The necessary
needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL PCR
tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of
the cells from the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were
added for the collection of the picked cells. Depending on the cells
picked they either contained 20 µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris, 6 M Urea,
protease and phosphatase inhibitors, pH 7.5-8) or TFE lysis buffer ().
When everything was ready, the needle holder was placed into the right
slide holder position in the machine.

Next, the Imager software was opened, and the calibration of the needle
was performed. It allows precise control over it by aligning the actual
position (x, y) of the needle with the position displayed in the Imager

, and the calibration of the needle
was performed. It allows precise control over it by aligning the actual
position (x, y) of the needle with the position displayed in the Imager
and by assuring that the tip of the needle actually touches the slide
(z) during the further picking process. For the calibration, an empty
slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide holder
position. The fingerprints helped with focusing of the image. Then a
needle was selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing
solution. Afterwards the needle was positioned above the slide for
either an automatic calibration or a manual one, where the parameters of
the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were adjusted.

In a last step, the created .rcpickmap file that withholds the selected
ROIs was imported into the Imager software with the Imager Main Control
together with the .metadata file of the slide.

**Collection Plates (4titude)**

To compare the performance of the two plate types and identify the
origin of polymer contamination, collections will be divided into the
following conditions:

 - **Contents:** Laser-ablated material from the empty PEN slide,

 > background signals specifically generated by the PEN polymer

document_chunks[38].chunk_text: # LCM-Proteomics Pipeline: Troubleshooting and DVP Replication

## 1. Root Cause Analysis: Low Protein Yield and Polymer Contamination

The previous experimental run experienced significant polymer
contamination and suboptimal protein identification. When comparing the
executed workflow against the Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) pipeline,
several critical discrepancies emerge as the primary sources of these
issues:

- **Plate Material and High-Heat Decrosslinking:** Standard 4titude
 > 96-well PCR plates were utilized. These plates are manufactured
 > with plasticizers and mold-release agents. Subjecting these plates
 > to high-heat FFPE thermal decrosslinking in the presence of urea
 > aggressively leached these polymers into the microscopic sample
 > volume, causing massive mass spectrometer interference.
 > Furthermore, standard polypropylene intrinsically binds proteins,

leached these polymers into the microscopic sample
 > volume, causing massive mass spectrometer interference.
 > Furthermore, standard polypropylene intrinsically binds proteins,
 > leading to heavy sample loss when dealing with low-abundance
 > inputs.

- **Slide Membrane Chemistry:** The use of PET (Polyethylene
 > terephthalate) membranes rather than PEN (Polyethylene
 > naphthalate) or PPS (Polyphenylene sulfide) contributed to the
 > background.

- **Buffer Selection:** Collecting directly into 20 μL of
 > high-concentration urea is chemically aggressive. The DVP pipeline
 > utilizes Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC), which is a volatile,
 > MS-compatible buffer that evaporates cleanly during vacuum drying
 > without stripping plastics or leaving residual salts.

- [**Commercial Blocking Buffers:** The application of proprietary
 > blockers (like Odyssey) during staining introduces exogenous
 > proteins (e.g., casein, BSA) and polymers that can mask the
 > delicate endogenous peptide signals of the tissue.]{.mark}

In Lina's experiments - PCR tubes were utilized, FG-021 (Nippon
Genetics)

## 2. Protocol Comparison: Executed Experiment vs. DVP Standard

### A. Refined Summary of the Previously Performed Experiment (Helsinki Lab)

Samples were sectioned onto metal-frame PET membrane slides (pretreated
with UV-light) and baked at 60°C. Staining was performed using a custom

erformed Experiment (Helsinki Lab)

Samples were sectioned onto metal-frame PET membrane slides (pretreated
with UV-light) and baked at 60°C. Staining was performed using a custom
fluorescence panel and a commercial Odyssey blocking buffer. To minimize
tissue detachment risks, aggressive pressure-cooker antigen retrieval
was bypassed. Laser capture microdissection (LCM) was conducted at FIMM
utilizing fluorescence guidance to bypass lengthy Whole Slide Imaging
(WSI). Dissected tissue elements were catapulted directly into a
standard 4titude 96-well PCR plate pre-filled with 20 μL of urea buffer.
Following collection, the plate was snap-frozen, shipped for mass
spectrometry, and subjected to FFPE thermal decrosslinking, resulting in
extensive polymer leaching.

### B. Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) Pipeline (Lengyel Lab)

FFPE tissues are sectioned onto 1.0 PEN membrane slides pre-treated with
UV-light and VECTABOND reagent to ensure tissue adhesion during harsh
treatments. Following deparaffinization and rehydration, samples undergo
heat-induced epitope retrieval and are stained avoiding complex protein
blockers. High-resolution imaging and machine-learning-driven
segmentation define the cutting contours. The LCM catapults designated
cell populations into *dry*, empty wells of an MS-grade 384-well plate
(e.g., Eppendorf Protein LoBind). In-well lysis and thermal

e cutting contours. The LCM catapults designated
cell populations into *dry*, empty wells of an MS-grade 384-well plate
(e.g., Eppendorf Protein LoBind). In-well lysis and thermal
decrosslinking are executed simultaneously using an Ammonium Bicarbonate
(ABC) buffer at 95°C. Digestion follows using a mixture of LysC and
Trypsin in an ABC/Acetonitrile buffer. Finally, peptides are
vacuum-dried, resuspended, and injected directly into the LC-MS via an
autosampler.

## 3. Checklist for Future DVP-Aligned Experiments

## 4. Standard Operating Procedure: Replicating the DVP Pipeline

1. **Slide Preparation:** Treat 1.0 PEN membrane slides with VECTABOND.
 > Mount FFPE sections (5-7 μm) and bake to ensure adherence.

2. **Deparaffinization & Staining:** Deparaffinize using xylene and
 > rehydrate through graded ethanol. Perform necessary antigen

sections (5-7 μm) and bake to ensure adherence.

2. **Deparaffinization & Staining:** Deparaffinize using xylene and
 > rehydrate through graded ethanol. Perform necessary antigen
 > retrieval and immunofluorescence staining. Strictly avoid complex,
 > undefined commercial blocking agents.

3. **Laser Capture Microdissection (LCM):** Target the defined regions
 > of interest (e.g., HGSC subsister cells). Dissect and catapult the
 > tissue directly into the dry wells of an Eppendorf 384-well
 > Protein LoBind plate.

4. **In-Well Lysis and Decrosslinking:** Centrifuge the plate briefly
 > to ensure all tissue is at the bottom. Add the MS-compatible lysis
 > buffer (e.g., 20 μL of Ammonium Bicarbonate). Seal the plate
 > tightly and heat at 95°C for 60 minutes to decrosslink the FFPE
 > matrix.

5. **Enzymatic Digestion:** Cool the plate. Add the secondary digestion
 > buffer (e.g., Acetonitrile in ABC) and heat briefly at 75°C.
 > Introduce LysC and Trypsin for overnight digestion at 37°C.

document_chunks[41].chunk_text: **Student:** MSc Nika Mikhailava

**Principal Investigator:** Professor Anniina Färkkilä

**Project Title:** **Single-cell spatial proteomic profiling of
subsister cell phenotypes in HGSC**

### Introduction & Preliminary Findings

First-line therapies in high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) often fail to
completely eradicate the tumor, leaving a reservoir of cells that
survive chemotherapy - termed \"**subsister cells**\". We hypothesize
that these spatially distinct and phenotypically heterogeneous cell
populations drive divergent clinical outcomes, including tumor relapse.

"**subsister cells**\". We hypothesize
that these spatially distinct and phenotypically heterogeneous cell
populations drive divergent clinical outcomes, including tumor relapse.

To investigate this, we performed highly multiplexed **t-CycIF (40
markers, n=27)** and **Xenium spatial transcriptomics (430 genes,
n=16)** on Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS) samples. Our preliminary
spatial analysis resolved these subsister cells into phenotypically
distinct and spatially segregated niches, identifying Highly
Proliferative, CCND1-dominant, and Heterogeneous clinically relevant
patient archetypes. Crucially, the enrichment of the **Highly
Proliferative niches (Ki67+/pRb+/pH3+)** significantly correlated with
shorter Progression-Free Intervals (PFI). This finding suggests that
these specific spatial niches actively drive tumor relapse.

### **Motivation for Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)**

While our targeted approaches (t-CycIF, Xenium) successfully mapped the
architecture of these niches, they are limited by predefined antibody
and gene panels. To discover actionable therapeutic vulnerabilities, we
need an unbiased characterization of their full proteomic landscape.
**Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)** perfectly bridges this gap, enabling
the precise excision of these specific subsister niches to uncover their
proteome in a spatially preserved context.

### Study Cohort

(DVP)** perfectly bridges this gap, enabling
the precise excision of these specific subsister niches to uncover their
proteome in a spatially preserved context.

### Study Cohort

DVP will be executed on FFPE tumor samples from **8-16 IDS patients**.
To evaluate the epithelial tumor compartments, we will select 3--6
regions of interest (ROIs) per tissue section, laser-microdissecting
approximately 200 tumor cells per ROI.

These samples were specifically chosen because they constitute our
\"integrated multi-omics\" cohort. By utilizing serial sections from
these exact patients, we can anchor the new DVP data directly to our
existing profiles (matched t-CycIF, Xenium, scRNA-seq, and WES). If
resources permit, spatial proteomic profiling will be expanded to
additional samples from our broader t-CycIF cohort **(up to 69 IDS
samples)**.

### Internship Aims & Tasks

**Main Aim:** **To explore the spatial proteomic landscape of clinically
relevant subsister niches.**

**Pre-arrival Phase (Helsinki):**

- Conduct pilot laser capture microdissection (LCM) to validate
 > protocols and assess infrastructure readiness.

- Explore clinical associations of subsister phenotypes with bulk
 > RNA-seq data.

**Internship Phase (Chicago):**

- Acquire hands-on technical proficiency in the complete DVP
 > methodology.

- Perform image-guided LCM to isolate predefined subsister niches from

Internship Phase (Chicago):**

- Acquire hands-on technical proficiency in the complete DVP
 > methodology.

- Perform image-guided LCM to isolate predefined subsister niches from
 > a scalable cohort ranging from 8 (multi-omics cohort) to 69
 > (t-CycIF cohort) patient samples.

- Conduct high-sensitivity mass spectrometry to comprehensively
 > profile the proteome of these isolated regions and define
 > targetable vulnerabilities.

## **Project Overview**

**Brief Description of Research Goals:\
**Refine image-derived quantified data through data normalization and
assignment of precise phenotype labels to the cells of the tumor
microenvironment (TME). Explore the composition of the TME by analyzing
the abundance of phenotypes across the cohort. Compare two different
spatial analysis methods---spatial count and latent Dirichlet allocation
(LDA)---using different parameters (radius: 13, 25, 35 microns) and
assessing their suitability for various types of analysis. Determine
which approach is better suited for specific analytical goals and
compare the results between distinct clinical groups to explore
potential differences and trends.

document_chunks[59].chunk_text: The nuclear accumulation of cyclin D1 is indicative of a high mitotic
index in cancer cells, and is considered to be a prognostic
marker.Cyclin D1 overexpression, as observed in cancer cells,
antagonizes the checkpoint-induced cell cycle arrest, disturbs DNA
replication and allows damaged DNA to be replicated

rker.Cyclin D1 overexpression, as observed in cancer cells,
antagonizes the checkpoint-induced cell cycle arrest, disturbs DNA
replication and allows damaged DNA to be replicated

[Cytoplasmic and membrane-associated cyclin D1 regulates tumor cells
invasion and dissemination]{.underline}.Cytoplasmic cyclin D1 associates
with structural proteins of the cytoskeleton, affecting cell
shape[[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6961668/]{.underline}](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6961668/)

HOW DOES IT WORK: Cyclin D1 expression requires the activation of
RAS-mediated signaling cascades, which is accompanied by
phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)/AKT-dependent CCND1 translation and a
decrease in cyclin D1 degradation. The active cyclin D1/CDK4/6 complexes
move to the nucleus, and cyclin D1 levels increase from early to late G1
phase, subsequently decreasing during S phase, when cyclin D1 is
exported into the cytoplasm for degradation by the ubiquitin-proteasome
system (UPS). This requires the phosphorylation of the threonine 286
(Figure 1(b)), which is catalyzed by, at least, three kinases: glycogen
synthase-3β (GSK3β), p38 MAP kinase (MAPK) and extracellular
signal-regulated kinases (ERK1/2) \[28,29\]. In the cytoplasm, the
ubiquitylation of phosphorylated cyclin D1 is dependent on a SCF complex

p38 MAP kinase (MAPK) and extracellular
signal-regulated kinases (ERK1/2) \[28,29\]. In the cytoplasm, the
ubiquitylation of phosphorylated cyclin D1 is dependent on a SCF complex
consisting of the SKP1 adaptor protein, the CUL1 scaffold protein and a
F-box proteins. Mutations or alterations of cyclin D1-specific E3
ubiquitin ligases contribute to cyclin D1 accumulation in cancers
\[33,34\]. In turn, [the promotion of cyclin D1 degradation suppresses
cancer cell growth]{.underline}. This can be achieved by the knockdown
of USP2 coding a cyclin D1-specific deubiquitylase or by the use of
inhibitors.

Cyclin D1 has a nuclear export signal and is a cargo of exportin 1
(XPO1). However, it has no nuclear localization signal and cannot enter
the nucleus on its own. Instead, its nuclear entry is favored both by
the CIP/KIP (CDK-interacting protein, kinase inhibitory protein) family
members and CDK4, and cyclin D1/CDK4/p21CIP1/p27KIP1 complexes are
imported into the nucleus upon activation

The Two-Faced Nature of p21 May Depend on its Subcellular Localization:

When in the nucleus, p21 can act as a tumor suppressor: it may inhibit
CKIs in G1 or G2/M cell cycle arrest initiated by p53; it may
downregulate many, but not all, DNA repair pathways; it can limit the
proliferation of stem cells; and it can also induce senescence
[\[10\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1471491417300205?via%3Dihub#bib0050).

When p21 is concentrated in the cytoplasm, cells with damaged or absent
p53 may acquire oncogenic properties: it may inhibit apoptosis,
facilitate cell migration, and promote cell proliferation

GSEA Hallmarks

![](media/image5.png){width="6.267716535433071in"
height="3.4722222222222223in"}

The GSEA Hallmarks dataset analysis revealed several key findings:

- **DNA Repair**: Upregulated, indicating a heightened need for DNA
 > repair mechanisms to support active progression through the S
 > phase. This is essential for cells to replicate DNA efficiently
 > during cell cycle progression.

- **MYC Targets V1 & V2**: Both pathways are upregulated, driving
 > active progression from G1 to S phase. These pathways are
 > associated with increased glucose uptake, altered oxidative
 > phosphorylation, and the promotion of cancer stem-like properties.

ession from G1 to S phase. These pathways are
 > associated with increased glucose uptake, altered oxidative
 > phosphorylation, and the promotion of cancer stem-like properties.

- **MTORC1 Signaling**: Promotes cell growth and proliferation by
 > regulating cell cycle progression. Additionally, it influences
 > glucose metabolism and immune suppression.

- **E2F Targets**: These targets are active when phosphorylated Rb
 > protein fails to inhibit E2F, further driving the transition from
 > G1 to S phase.

- **G2M Checkpoint**: Indicates activation of processes facilitating
 > the transition into the G2 phase, supporting cell cycle
 > progression.

Overall, the analysis suggests active cell cycle progression,
particularly the transition from G1 to S phase, driven by various
molecular pathways involved in growth, metabolism, and DNA repair.

Upregulation of the **Minichromosome Maintenance (MCM)** pathway
indicates active DNA replication. MCM proteins are essential for the
formation of replication forks, DNA unwinding, and initiating
replication.

**Double-stranded break repair** via break-induced replication is
activated when replication forks collapse during DNA replication,
facilitating repair mechanisms to maintain genomic integrity.

**Chromosome condensation** and **kinetochore organization** suggest
active preparation for mitosis, ensuring proper chromosome alignment and
segregation.

**K6-linked ubiquitination** regulates BRCA1/2-mediated DNA repair,
specifically in double-stranded break (DSB) repair through homologous
recombination, critical for maintaining genome stability during cell
division.

**Expansion CyCIF**

Experiment planning:

OncosysOVA IDS samples were taken. If the samples were used in GeoMX or
Xenium experiments, they were automatically included in the expansion
CyCIF. To homogenize the cohort, omental samples were chosen for the
rest of the cohort. Then, omental samples were checked for quality
through HE stainings. If the tumor purity on HE staining was more than
10% and no hemorrhage or tumor necrosis was observed, the tissue was
added to the experiment.

![](media/image11.jpg){width="5.291666666666667in"
height="5.337132545931759in"}

A total of 72 samples were included in the expanded CyCIF analysis. Of
these, 27 were previously used in the discovery CyCIF.

Tissues were sourced as follows: 47 from GeoMx's prior experiments, 8
from recent Xenium cuts, and 17 freshly cut prior to the experiment.

Regarding homologous recombination (HR) status: 11 tissues were
HR-deficient, 10 had an undefined HR status, and 51 were HR-proficient.

![Screenshot 2025-09-15 at

he experiment.

Regarding homologous recombination (HR) status: 11 tissues were
HR-deficient, 10 had an undefined HR status, and 51 were HR-proficient.

![Screenshot 2025-09-15 at

height="2.9166666666666665in"}

The main objective of expanding tCyCIF is to enable deep phenotyping and
mapping of cancer cell cycle states across a larger cohort. By
increasing the patient sample size, we aim to gain a better
understanding of clinical associations and enhance phenotyping of cell
cycle states using an improved functional panel. With the extended
cohort, we will also be able to integrate multiple data modalities,
including spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA sequencing, and genomics.
